Israel's Space Agency and France's National Centre for Space Studies (CNES) have teamed up to develop new satellites that will be able to investigate this deep cloud phenomenon, with Professor Rosenfeld as its principal investigator.<br/><br/>Either way, the conclusion is the same. Our current global climate predictions do not correctly take into account the significant effects of aerosols on clouds on Earth's overall energy balance. Further, Rosenfeld's recalculations mean fellow scientists will have to rethink their global warming predictions — which currently predict a 1.5 to 4.5-degree Celsius temperature increase by the end of the 21st century — to provide us a more accurate diagnosis — and prognosis — of the Earth's climate.<br/><br/>What we don't have, currently, is a political and economic system that allows us to respond to the demands of our time. We cannot afford to have global production, distribution, and human movement to be dictated by and for the benefit of a tiny fraction of humanity. The drive for endless growth, and endless capital accumulation, even if we did have a more progressive system of taxation and wealth re-distribution, creates artificial scarcity, and prevents the development of a society that can be sustained in the long term.<br/><br/>This is not just an emergency created by our use of energy, it's also rooted in the capitalist system that currently governs nearly all human activity on this planet. We need radical democracy in politics and in economies if we're to have any hope of addressing our environmental crisis, and of saving humanity. We need it soon was originally posted in February of 2016, before I came to Freethoughtblogs. I'm reposting it here because I think it's worth having around, and because it's relevant to a post I'm currently working on relating to global dimming.<br/><br/>Over the last couple decades, the world's business and political leaders have gradually come to understand that climate change is something that cannot be ignored. Every year, the immediacy and severity of the problem have become clearer. Sea level rise, seasonal changes, and even evolutionary changes in response to the rise in planetary temperature have all made it clear that the entire planet is changing around us, and that ignoring it could have devastating results.<br/><br/>Living, as we do, in a society that values money so highly, some of the responses have been predictable. In particular, businesspeople like Bill Gates have been pushing the idea of geoengineering as a solution. Geoengineering, in this context, is a catch-all phrase for deliberately tinkering with Earth's climate and the mechanisms that affect it. The problem with this is that the term is so broad it's almost useless. It can apply to things like planting more trees, and it can also apply to colossal structures in space to reduce incoming sunlight.<br/><br/>One of the most commonly discussed geoengineering solutions is iron fertilization of the ocean. The basic idea is simple – iron is a limiting nutrient in the ocean, so putting iron particles in the ocean will stimulate the growth of photosynthetic plankton, which will pull CO2 out of the atmosphere. The idea is that when the plankton die, a sizable amount of their mass will sink to the bottom of the ocean taking that carbon with it.<br/><br/>Newly published research now indicates that because iron is not the only low-availability nutrient in the ocean, the algal bloom from iron fertilization in one part of the ocean might pull other nutrients, like nitrates and phosphates, out of the water, starving plankton farther downstream along the oceanic currents.<br/><br/>It's tempting to simply wave away geoengineering as a bad idea that we should bury and be done with. There are countless ways that it could go horribly wrong, especially when enacted by billionaires like Gates and his ilk, who have little to no understanding of the ecosystems with which they want to tamper. With the possible exception of planting more trees and creating more wild spaces (which would, without question, work), pretty much every proposal for geoengineering has the potential to have devastating side effects that could make life on Earth much more difficult.<br/><br/>There's one compelling reason not to throw it away altogether. The reality is that we are already engaged in geoengineering, and there is no question that the path we're currently on will end badly. Like it or not, humanity has become a force of nature. The size of our population and the scale of our technology mean that we exert a global influence of the chemical makeup of our planet's oceans, atmosphere, land masses. Currently, we are engaged in the kind of geoengineering that Svante Arrhenius calculated was possible over a century ago – raising the planet's temperature by increasing greenhouse gas concentrations.<br/><br/>For the sake of our own long-term survival, not to mention the rest of life on Earth, we need to come to terms with the fact that our species exerts a global influence, and we need to take deliberate control of that influence. We are already geoengineers, we're just not taking responsibility for it. It's past time to do more than simply work on reducing our fossil fuel use – we need to think about how we manage the surface of the planet we live on, and how we can manage it for the benefit of all life on Earth – ourselves included.<br/><br/>Because right now, we still seem to be pretending that we can just stop having a planetary impact, and with our population headed for 10 billion in just a couple decades, that is the one option that is no longer available to us name of my blog implies, I have some interest in the amount of dissolved oxygen in the ocean. It's not a subject about which I have any particular expertise, it's just something that can have a dramatic effect on aquatic ecosystems, and which relates to one of the possible long-term "nightmare scenarios" for global warming.<br/><br/>When it comes to the basic facts of climate change, there are a few things that are really quite straightforward, despite the overall complexity of the issue. The properties of CO2, as described by Foote in 1856 mean that an increase in CO2 levels in the atmosphere will cause an increase in global temperature. The only question is how much. Arrhenius answered that question in 1896 with a general calculation of how much affect a given change in CO2 would have on Earth's overall temperature. So we know that increasing CO2 levels increases the planet's temperature, and we know roughly how much we'll get from a given increase. There's uncertainty there, in part because of the complexity of Earth's climate system. Amplifying feedback loops can increase greenhouse gas levels, and suppressing feedback loops might decrease them. But overall, if the atmospheric CO2 level goes up, the temperature must also rise.<br/><br/>Likewise, if the temperature rises, water will be able to hold less dissolved oxygen. This is also something we've known for a long time, although I've had more trouble tracking down the earliest research on it. The earliest references I've found were poorly cited, but in the 1940s. If anybody has more detail on that, I'll welcome the addition!<br/><br/>We can start with dead zones. A dead zone is an area in a body of water in which the dissolved oxygen levels have gotten too low to support most multicellular life. There are a number of these, increasingly well publicized, around the world's coasts. Most are caused by human activity. Not all of them exist year-round, but the effects of annual oxygen depletion outlast the depletion itself. These are typically caused by eutrophication (an increase in limiting nutrients) as a result of farm runoff flowing into the sea. The one that forms around the Mississippi delta every spring for this reason is probably the most famous one in the U.S. The influx of fertilizer causes an algae bloom, which can cause a brief increase in oxygen during the day, but then at night, respiring algae suck the air out of the room, so to speak. If they are able, fish will leave the area. If they can't, either because of the size of it, or because they can't tell which way to go, they will die. Less mobile animals tend to simply die.<br/><br/>It's important to note that these dead zones are not caused by an increase in temperature, and the oxygen levels rise after the nutrient influx stops, and the algal bloom goes away. The decrease in oxygen due to rising temperatures may start showing in seasonal patterns, but it won't be as dramatic. The network of currents throughout the planet's oceanic system move water around too much to allow for a global state of anoxia at any point in the near future. As I wrote at the beginning of my blogging career, there are ways in which a majority of the ocean could end up as a dead zone. That scenario is one of the possible major factors in the biggest mass extinction in the geological record. Rising temperatures would contribute to that happening, of course, but they couldn't cause it by themselves without first causing a dramatic change in global oceanic currents would be needed to stop mixing between layers of the ocean.<br/><br/>From Livescience article linked to the image: Low-oxygen zones are spreading around the globe. Red dots mark coastal locations where oxygen has plummeted to 2 milligrams per liter or less, and blue areas mark zones with the same low-oxygen levels in the open ocean. (Image credit: GO2NE working group. Data from World Ocean Atlas 2013 and provided by R. J. Diaz)<br/><br/>So what are we likely to see in our lifetimes? The speed of warming has been increasing over the years, and that will continue until some time after we start treating climate change like the global emergency it is. Barring major changes in how fertilizers are used in agriculture, dead zones at river deltas will continue to be a thing, and are likely to get worse with higher temperatures. The hypoxic zone in the equatorial Atlantic has given us a look at the first effects of decreasing oxygen:<br/><br/>An expanding zone of low oxygen, known as a hypoxic zone, in the Atlantic Ocean is encroaching upon these species' preferred oxygen-abundant habitat, forcing them into shallower waters where they are more likely to be caught.<br/><br/>During the study, published recently in the journal Fisheries Oceanography, scientists tagged 79 sailfish and blue marlin with satellite tracking devices in the western North Atlantic, off south Florida and the Caribbean; and eastern tropical Atlantic, off the coast of West Africa. The pop off archival satellite tags monitored horizontal and vertical movement patterns. Researchers confirmed that billfish prefer oxygen rich waters closer to the surface and will actively avoid waters low in oxygen.<br/><br/>While these hypoxic zones occur naturally in many areas of the world's tropical and equatorial oceans, scientists are concerned because these zones are expanding and occurring closer to the sea surface, and are expected to continue to grow as sea temperatures rise.<br/><br/>"The hypoxic zone off West Africa, which covers virtually all the equatorial waters in the Atlantic Ocean, is roughly the size of the continental United States, and it's growing," said Dr. Eric D. Prince, NOAA's Fisheries Service research fishery biologist. "With the current cycle of climate change and accelerated global warming, we expect the size of this zone to increase, further reducing the available habitat for these fish."<br/><br/>Less available habitat can lead to more fish being caught since the fish are concentrated near the surface. Higher catch rates from these areas may give the false appearance of more abundant fish stocks. The shrinking availability of habitat and resulting increases to catch rates are important factors for scientists to consider when doing population assessments.<br/><br/>This is probably going to be the primary effect we see in our lifetimes. Just as fish are shifting ranges and patterns due to temperature preferences, they'll also move in response to changing oxygen levels, and to the activities of other fish. Combined with the ever-present threat of overfishing, this could lead to a number of fishery collapses in the near future, affecting the global food supply at the same time as our agricultural systems are struggling to adapt to changes in weather patterns and water availability.<br/><br/>What's more, their study found that the marine biosphere—the chain of sea life anchored by phytoplankton—invests around one percent (1 terawatt) of its chemical power fortune in mechanical energy, which is manifested in the swimming motions of hungry ocean swimmers ranging from whales and fish to shrimp and krill. Those swimming motions mix the water much as cream is stirred into coffee by swiping a spoon through it.<br/><br/>And the sum of all that phytoplankton-fueled stirring may equal climate control.<br/><br/>"By interpreting existing data in a different way, we have predicted theoretically that the amount of mixing caused by ocean swimmers is comparable to the deep ocean mixing caused by the wind blowing on the ocean surface and the effects of the tides," Dewar said.<br/><br/>In fact, he explained, biosphere mixing appears to provide about one third the power required to bring the deep, cold waters of the world ocean to the surface, which in turn completes the ocean's conveyor belt circulation critical to the global climate system.<br/><br/>Findings from the FSU-led study ("Does the marine biosphere mix the ocean?") will appear in the forthcoming issue of the Journal of Marine Research, adding the role of major power broker to phytoplankton's already impressive credentials.<br/><br/>Scientists for some time have known that the highly sensitive plants act as reliable signals of environmental changes at or near the ocean surface through sudden declines or rapid growth—and they have suspected that phytoplankton affect as well as reflect climate change when large, sustained plant populations gulp carbon dioxide from the atmosphere during grand-scale photosynthesis.<br/><br/>But along with the new calculations that point to the marine biosphere's bigger-than-expected role in ocean mixing and climate control, Dewar and his colleagues also suggest that human and environmental decimation of whale and big fish populations may have had a measurable impact on the total biomixing occurring in the world's oceans.<br/><br/>The biggest danger the oceanic ecosystem faces from warming water probably isn't going to be a decrease in dissolved oxygen, but rather damage to the plankton that forms the base for most of the oceans' food webs. That may be compounded by big fish like tuna being concentrated away from hypoxic zones, but the decline in available food would be a problem even without changes to oxygen levels.<br/><br/>The plankton decline will also probably give us an increased number of headlines about declining oxygen production from the oceans, as the article I linked indicates. That said, the bigger danger from that, in my estimation, is not that we'll run of of oxygen to breathe, but that we'll lose the ecosystem service of CO2 uptake provided by phytoplankton.<br/><br/>The global terraforming experiment we're currently running is a bit mind-boggling, really. When we get over-the-top headlines, they're sensationalist in focus, not in scale – concerns about running out of oxygen this century, or the scenario that gave birth to the title of this blog aren't reasonable in our lifetimes, and for that I'm grateful. The reality, however, is just as dramatic, and while it's hard to predict exactly how things will play out, I think it's safe to say that it will continue to feel like we're caught in a cycle of escalating chaos.<br/><br/>Because we areAlexandria Ocasio-Cortez has been an exciting figure for a number of reasons, but I think the biggest one is her commitment to relying on small-dollar donors, and a "sneakers on the ground" approach to campaigning. On the one hand, it's nice to feel that we have some legislators who don't owe anything to the aristocrats, but I think it goes beyond that.<br/><br/>All those big-dollar donations don't just mean that the politicians "owe" their donors, they also represent a huge amount of time spent begging for that money. That's time not spent talking or listening to constituents. That's time not spent studying the topics on which they're passing laws. That's time not spent writing legislation. That's time not spent researching the people they're going to be talking to in congressional hearings.<br/><br/>It's been refreshing, over the last couple years, to see members of Congress like AOC, Katie Porter, Ilhan Omar, Ayanna Pressley, and others asking difficult, informed questions of agency heads, corporate executives, and other folks brought in under the guise of helping Congress inform themselves about the laws they're crafting and passing.<br/><br/>I don't think it's a coincidence that those questions seem to be mostly coming from the more left-wing members of the Democratic Party, while those to the center and the right tend to make for less interesting viewing, less pressing questions, and more empty grandstanding. Part of that is about priorities, of course. Politicians further to the right on the political spectrum don't have the same goals for the nature and effectiveness of the U.S. government.<br/><br/>Part of it, though, goes back to the time spent fundraising. AOC isn't just naturally more knowledgeable about those subjects. She and her colleagues are so effective because they actually put in the time studying the subjects, and listening to experts, and crafting useful questions. Their approach to funding their political campaigns doesn't just give them the rhetorical benefits of Left populism, it also buys them the time that's needed to actually be effective at their jobs.<br/><br/>I think there are lessons there for people beyond the realm of politics. As I and my readers have mentioned before, the use of working-class power costs us more, and is more diffuse that the use of capitalist power. It takes more time, effort, and sacrifice to bring our power to bear and to keep it focused on the tasks at hand. That makes the "easy" solution of taking capitalist funding very, very tempting. On the surface, at least, money given by some large capitalist entity, be it an organization or an individual, can make things a lot easier. It can mean much-needed resources going towards good causes. It can also, however, have a corrupting effect beyond the conventional story of people getting greedy, and losing sight of the mission.<br/><br/>Massive funding from a small number of "well-meaning" wealthy sources can mean that, on the surface, there's a sense that time and effort spent securing those big donations gets a better return for the cause, and that can, with the best of intentions, result in starving the aspects of our organizations that not only seek funding from "the people", but that also put those organizations in constant contact with those people.<br/><br/>And in time, becoming reliant on the support of the capitalist/aristocratic class is more likely than not to turn into the kind of subservient patronage relationship we see all to often in Congress.<br/><br/>The issue of material resources is massively important for any revolution worth having, regardless of whether that revolution is violent or nonviolent. Unfortunately, where those resources come from seems to matter beyond vague notions of purity.<br/><br/>This video from Space Commune digs into these questions in a way that I think makes it required watching for anyone who believes that people should be able to govern themselves:A small Siberian town north of the Arctic Circle reached 100.4 degrees Fahrenheit on Saturday, a figure that—if verified—would be the highest temperature reading in the region since record-keeping began in 1885.With all the chaos and misery of the last four years, it's hard not to feel as if we've entered into the "climate catastrophe" era I always feared. It has always been clear that the climate has been warming faster than most scientists have predicted (despite what the science deniers keep saying), and I've always known, in the back of my mind, that global warming and associated chaos would be the "setting" for the second half of my life. It was a gloomy enough prospect that I generally focused on the slim hope that it could be avoided, but I don't think it's a secret that I've been expecting we'd keep falling short of the various changes needed.<br/><br/>Earth's systems are already out of balance. The comparative equilibrium we saw during most of the last 10,000 years meant that the amount of ice we had was roughly the amount of ice we were likely to get and keep at our current temperature and greenhouse gas level. When we increased the average temperature, that balance was shifted, and ice started melting in response to the increased temperature of the climate.<br/><br/>The "lull" between 1998 and 2015, which was not much of a lull, still saw accelerating ice melt, permafrost thawing, and sea level rise, because we had already raised the temperature enough to make those inevitable, based on our understanding of physics. Even a year that was down to the 1990 or 1980 temperature level, on average, followed by a return to 2000s temperatures, would have fairly little effect. The melting would have slowed, without stopping, and then sped up again when the temperature returned to the decadal "norm".<br/><br/>But a dramatically hotter year – like this El Niño year – is a different matter. It injects a bunch more heat into the system, which means faster ice melt, and so lower albedo for the coming year, and more permafrost melt, and so more greenhouse gasses for the coming year, and more water evaporation, and so more greenhouse gasses for the coming year.<br/><br/>A single, unusually cold year, does not do much when we're still above the temperature at which the current ice sheets formed, but a single hot year can create a spike of warming factors, which will cause even more warming in the years to come.<br/><br/>If we had not been emitting fossil fuels, it's possible that the dip in global temperatures in the late 1960s/early 1970s would have led to more global cooling, and even an ice age – we're certainly due for one – but we had already started the slowly accelerating process of global warming. We already had warming momentum, even back then, so we had a temporary cool period, and then when we came out of the 1970s, the temperature skyrocketed.<br/><br/>We'll have more warming "pauses" in the future. That is a virtual certainty, but unless we re-balance the planet's temperature budget by reducing greenhouse gases, the planet will just keep warming until it reaches a new equilibrium. Because of feedbacks like the albedo and the melting permafrost, even if we stop emitting CO2 now, the planet will keep warming for thousands of years, and the new equilibrium will be far, far hotter than anything our species has ever encountered.<br/><br/>There are a number of ways we could respond to this, but our best bet is to stop contributing to the problem, prepare for the changes we know are coming, and develop a strategy for deliberately managing the planet's greenhouse gas levels.<br/><br/>That said, there is one way in which I am an optimist on this. I still believe that, through science, technology, and massive social and economic change, we can weather the coming storm, and even thrive as a species, while helping the rest of the planet recover from the damage we've done. That said, I believe it's safe to say that we can now no longer avoid a period of chaos and hardship unlike anything humanity has ever experienced. That's one of the biggest reasons that it's so important to address injustices like those created by white supremacy. We've got a lot of work to do, and we don't have time for this bigoted bullshit. The global fascist/white supremacist movement is acting massively amplify every other problem we face, kinda like climate change…Revolutions of all sorts have a common problem. It's one thing to build popular support for a change, it's another to actually get the power to create that change, and to make it last. Many of the struggles that underlie the current uprising in the United States are ones that have been going on for multiple lifetimes, in one form or another, and while there have been successes, they've often been less than was hoped for or promised, and followed by failures and rollbacks. This wasn't the post I planned on writing for Juneteenth, but there are lessons from the end of slavery that we would be wise to consider in the context of today's struggles.<br/><br/>Slavery ended after centuries of uprisings, struggles, and activism. The efforts to address the injustices of slavery were halted almost immediately, and the laws and economy of the United States were arranged to force the Black population to stay as third-class citizens, or to use the prison system to enslave them again. These efforts were accompanied by a generations-long campaign of terrorism and pogroms waged by the white population against the black population, led by the KKK, and supported by many who never wore a white hood. Along with that came an intensification of the anti-black propaganda that had been used to justify slavery, and was now served to justify any and all injustices perpetrated against the Black population.<br/><br/>It is very, very hard to take any power from the powerful. For all people like to talk about how the wealth of today's billionaires is somehow fictional because it's mostly tied up in stocks, the difficulty of getting changes that put even a fractional amount more power in the hands of the people shows just how real their fortunes are. They really do wield Feudal levels of power and influence, on a global scale, and they are prepared, as they always have been, to use that to hold on to what they view as "theirs", and to create a society in which each of the lower tiers of power take that same approach to whatever lesser degrees of power they can attain.<br/><br/>That dynamic is why so many have taken the path of bloody revolution. Any minor gains made are rolled back by the power that is left to the aristocracy, as they dream about how much more power they used to have. It's not hard to feel that the only solution is to take away all of their power, and to take away their lives, either through death or through imprisonment. I don't want that, I just don't want them to have any more power than anyone else. I want democracy. I want a system in which accumulating personal wealth isn't necessary to pursue a fulfilling life. I want a system in which accumulating personal power is either impossible, or close to it.<br/><br/>We're a long, long way off from that. I believe it's attainable, but it seems so far away that I doubt it will be achieved in my lifetime. That means that we're in this for the long haul. Even if we make changes that are big enough and fast enough to head off the worst horrors of a warming planet, we will need to hold that ground, not just against the capitalists seeking to regain their hegemony, but also against an increasingly hostile planet. Climate change has been described as a "threat multiplier", and I think that will prove true for the threat posed by the aristocrats and oligarchs of the world as much as any other.<br/><br/>The struggle against white supremacy is inextricably tied up in the struggle against global capitalism. The two are used to reinforce each other, as they have been for centuries. The working classes are powerful too, because there are more of us, and because we form the foundations of the power of the aristocracy. In a very simplistic equation, we have more power, but it's harder to use. The wealth of the "elites" is a ranged weapon that can be used without real risk to its wielder. They pay others to take their risks. The power of the working class is in our hands, and while we can use it to achieve great things, the risks we take are our own. A punch can be effective, but it can easily injure the one who throws it. That's what creates the imbalance – using our power comes at a greater cost, and greater risk, and that creates an imbalance.<br/><br/>Part of that power imbalance is the way the corporate media will largely ignore uprisings or protests until there's violence involved, and then they'll focus on the violence until someone decides it's not "new" enough. It feels like coverage has already started to shift away from the efforts for real change, and politicians who are supposedly on "our side" have started declaring victory due to the promise of very minor reforms. The weight of society is set against change, and while sometimes it can feel as if we've gained momentum, we're still engaged in an uphill struggle. For those of us who want to realize the dream of democracy and justice for all, I think it will always be an uphill struggle, as there will always be some trying to control others, at every scale of society. As the protests dwindle, and fade away, there will be a concerted effort by some to claim that they "won" and so there's no need for more protests in the future, and by others that they "lost" and so all future protests are doomed to failure or unpopular. The general refrain is always that while past struggles for change might have been justified and good, present and future ones are almost always bad, unpopular, and the work of extremists.<br/><br/>Hopefully this will result in more sustainable change and activism than the first wave of the Black Lives Matter movement, and understanding what's going on from a systemic perspective should help us make that happen. This video takes an analytical look at movements, the predictable patterns in how they progress, and how to use that knowledge to guide our efforts.<br/><br/>This pattern can be found not just in the short-term uprisings seen through things like Occupy Wall Street or Black Lives Matter, but also in much longer time frames. Today we celebrate one of the greatest victories in the centuries-long fight for freedom, justice, and equality. Not the end of the the American Civil War, or the Emancipation Proclamation that helped win it, but the end of industrialized, birthright slavery in the United States. It was not a total victory, but it moved us closer to justice, and made possible the victories gained since, and those still to comeAt the end of eighth grade, my class took a trip to Nova Scotia, in Canada. I don't remember exactly where we stayed, and I don't even remember a whole lot of the trip. I remember bits and pieces of the cruise we took up there from Maine, of playing Manhunt (a sort of combination between hide and seek and tag) around the buildings we were staying in, a campfire near the shore, and a Mortal Kombat-style arcade game involving dinosaurs. There's one memory that stands out more than the others, and it was the first time I told a ghost story and the experience really clicked with me. The story I told was The Red Lodge, by H.R. Wakefield. It had been published in a collection called "More Tales to Tremble By", and it had stuck with me for a variety of reasons. Reading it later, as an adult, revealed it to be… less good than I remembered. I don't know anything about Wakefield, but the narrator of the story comes across as both an author insert character, and as cartoonishly pretentious. My telling was not verbatim by any stretch, just as close to the original as I could construct by memory. I told the story to my classmates, including a few spooky moments with silhouettes and faces in windows, and during the silence after I finished, there was a noise, and two shadows appeared on the frosted glass window of the outer door. If memory serves, it was a couple chaperones coming to join the storytelling, but for a moment it was as if the specters from my story had manifested in reality. It was the moment when I discovered my love of storytelling.<br/><br/>For a long time, my storytelling and fiction writing focused on various horror stories. I had some friends who'd get together weekly in college for ghost stories, and I periodically have Halloween parties that center around sharing various spooky and supernatural stories. When I started trying to learn fiction writing as a craft, I started with horror. As I worked, I came to think of writing in terms of the emotions it could conjure in the reader, and to think about various techniques. Ideally, I want to guide my readers through a landscape that doesn't just contain a narrative of events, but also a variety of emotional experiences. I can't really say how skilled I am at my craft, but I think it's safe to say that with practice and study, I'm better than I was a decade ago. That's not much, but it's progress I can see, and I feel good about that.<br/><br/>One of the things I've learned is that some emotions are much easier to conjure and manipulate than others.<br/><br/>Using only words on paper (or a screen, or braille), how do you generate a feeling of surprised elation? How do you make someone feel hope? It's easy to write about someone feeling those things, but to actually reliably make a reader feel them seems more difficult, at least for me. Satisfaction, awe, comfort, the feeling of doing something for the first time – humanity is blessed with a nearly endless spectrum of ways to experience the world, and some of them are very difficult to replicate outside of simply living the events that create them.<br/><br/>In my experience, the easiest ones are things like fear and disgust. Our reactions to threats are pretty universal, and pretty near the surface because they generally come from a need for some immediate action. Get away from the scary thing. Wash off the gross thing. It could hurt us. It could make us sick. Pretty much everybody has had some version of those feelings, and they tend to generate strong memories.<br/><br/>That means they're also very easy to use in politics. It's why various forms of fear-mongering tend to work so well, and why there's so much focus on what some like to call "base instincts" or "primal instincts". Triggering emotional states that demand immediate action puts other instincts and needs on hold, and if you can maintain those feelings in a group of people, it's far easier to get them to move in the direction that you claim will make those feelings go away. It's a nasty tactic, because it always works, and because there are real problems in the world.<br/><br/>It's particularly vicious when it's used by the people who create those problems in the first place. In atheist discourse, it's sometimes said that religion convinces you that you're poisoned (when you're not), then offers you the fake cure to the fake poison. There are a lot of ways in which modern politics are similar to that, except that very often the "poison" isn't fictional. The economic hardship that people suffer is very real, but we live in a system that makes it difficult to tell what exactly is causing it. That means that the people who constantly push for policies that make life worse for most people can then turn around and blame that misery on a convenient scapegoat, while offering a "cure" that, more often than not, will only make things worse.<br/><br/>Modern conservative politics, in the US and the UK, at least, amount to a vampire telling the villagers that their strange neck wounds and feelings of weakness are caused by a disease that makes them produce too much blood, and so the solution is bleeding, and the vampire will take the excess blood away as a service.<br/><br/>And maybe the exact nature of the problem changes. Maybe, in time, people notice that it doesn't seem to be a disease, so they'll find something else. We used to think it was a disease, but now it's those new, different-looking people who've moved to town. Sure, the problem pre-dates them, but there were always travelers coming through before, and strangers living in the nearby forest, so maybe it's THEM causing the problem. The solution is to give all the different-looking people to the vampire, and he'll deal with it from there. And when, after all the strangers are gone, and anyone who looks different has been eliminated, or scared off, well, the problem's still there. Maybe it's a disease. Our ancestors thought it was a disease, and it sure seems like things were better in the past, so maybe we should rely on their traditional wisdom, and return to the old ways, and start that bleeding ritual.<br/><br/>And so it keeps working until the people realize that no matter the exact nature or explanation of the problem, the solution proposed by the local ruler always seems to result in a lot of blood being given to him, in one form or another, and maybe that is the problem.<br/><br/>Maybe we should try doing without that ruler.<br/><br/>And that's when the spell can be broken.<br/><br/>I think we're close to such a moment now. The pandemic has created a situation in which the "leaders" who have been offering cures like "do capitalism harder" or "give more money to rich people" are now pushing us towards the even more immediate and scary danger of a plague. It looks like their intentions aren't so "pure" after all, and maybe those nasty weirdos who've been ranting about vampires for all these years have a point.<br/><br/>And as many of us stay home, and isolated, we're discovering that we've got other primal needs that go beyond the immediate desires for food, shelter, safety, and sex. We need community. We need other people, not just to fight against immediate threats, but because community is part of what it means to be human.<br/><br/>There's an old saying, with which everyone is no doubt familiar – money doesn't buy happiness. These days it's often used to tell poor people that they shouldn't look to material wealth to solve their problems. After all, there are plenty of unhappy rich people, so we should all seek happiness through other means. Try to just enjoy your work more. Maybe find a hobby during all that free time you have. Get more sleep. Meditate. Practice a religion. Find happiness in some way that doesn't mean rich people become less rich.<br/><br/>That saying is true. Money does not buy happiness. Having your material needs met does not buy happiness. What it buys is the freedom to pursue happiness. It buys us time to think about what actually does make us happy. It buys us time with other people, to use for things other than merely struggling to get by. It buys us time to ponder life, and practice skills, to enjoy music, and to play games.<br/><br/>It buys us time to tell stories, and to hear them told.<br/><br/>When we spend all of our time and energy simply on getting money, that's time and energy we don't have for pursuit of happiness. We live in a world of astounding abundance. We grow more food than the global human population is capable of eating. We have so much material wealth that we simply throw away things when they are less than perfect. And at the same time, people have to work endlessly just to make ends meet. It's a contradiction. Something is wrong, and everyone can tell, so explanations are constantly created, and justifications offered. And as the whole population suffers, and seems to be constantly moving from one fear to the next, the aristocracy is reaching ever-loftier heights of prosperity and excess.<br/><br/>And the plague has shown us that as well. As hundreds of thousands have died, and millions more grow ever closer to destitution, a tiny handful of people have been adding incomprehensible amounts of wealth to their hoards, and their servants have been working hard to make those easy emotional plays. They want us to be afraid, and disgusted, and angry. They want us to believe that conflict, terror, greed, and rage are the essence of humanity, and that we can only make the bad feelings stop by making the "bad people" go away. Immigrants, people of a different color, "anarchists", "antifa" – anyone who seems to be causing disruption, they must be the cause of all that misery you were feeling.<br/><br/>If we just get rid of them, things will get better.<br/><br/>Removing the socialists didn't help? Well, they must have had allies. They sure did seem to like those union types, so we'd better get rid of unions.<br/><br/>Removing the unions didn't help? Well, we've always said the immigrants were a problem, and there's still a lot of them around.<br/><br/>This is the natural progression as the hoarding of necessities creates artificial scarcity. It seems like there's not enough to go around, even though there's more now than there ever has been, so the problem must be that someone's taking it all. It must be those mean people who're always saying that they're being mistreated. Everything seemed fine before the strange people at the bottom of the boat started complaining about leaks.<br/><br/>And so we're distracted from those emotions that can lift us up, as a species. We're distracted from what makes us human, and told that what really makes us human is our aggression, and our lust, and our greed, but since that's not all YOU want out of life, because YOU are a good person, well, it must be those other people who aren't like US. WE just want to live our lives, but THEY are constantly demanding more. THEY can't get past their primal instincts. THEY will never be satisfied, so the solution is to remove them.<br/><br/>But it's a lie. Its the new musician blasting loud notes in your ear and telling you it's the essence of music. It's the pulp horror writer telling you that the disgust and fear they can make you feel is the essence of storytelling.<br/><br/>Those are valid and important emotions. They are real instincts that are absolutely part of being human. They can be used to save lives, to educate, and to entertain. Just as some people enjoy pain in sex or in athletic pursuits, so to can we enjoy fear, disgust, anger, and greed.<br/><br/>But in constantly telling ourselves that those are the core of humanity, and that a society driven by those instincts will be better for everyone, we are denying most of what we are. The Tumblr post that inspired this essay puts it well:<br/><br/>I believe that the pursuit of happiness should be a right afforded to all sentient beings, as much as possible, and throughout history it has always been those other, less "urgent" instincts that have brought us closer to that goal | EVENTS<br/><br/>Nuair a bhí mé i m’údar óg, d’fhreastail mé ar Scoil ba ea ceann de na rudaí a theastaigh uaim ná maisiú ealaíne ar na aistí éagsúla a scríobh mé. Bhí dualgas orainn ár gcuid oibre a shoilsiú, agus aithris á dhéanamh ar na manaigh Eorpacha a bhí ag cóipeáil téacsanna reiligiúnacha. De ghnáth fágadh sonraí beachta na maisiúcháin seo suas leis na scoláirí. Uaireanta bhí sé líníochtaí faint taobh thiar den téacs, uaireanta bhí sé grád dath ar fud na himill, agus uaireanta bhí sé doodles sna himill a bhaineann le nádúr an aiste.<br/><br/>Cad nach ndearna mé, bhí i ndáiríre a chaitheamh am i bhfad ag déanamh staidéir ar an feiniméan na imeall imeallacha. Dóibh siúd agaibh a bhfuil, is dócha go bhfuil tú faoi deara a lán de na seilidí.<br/><br/>Yea, bhí rudaí slimy crawl ar féar, agus poke a súile ag dom<br/><br/>Tá seilidí ag troid ridirí, seilidí ag cuimhneamh ar a ngnó féin, fiú an Hoversnas c ócáideach le fear céile an fhreastalaí:<br/><br/>Gach Hail an Hoversanil<br/><br/>Tá teoiricí éagsúla ann maidir le cén fáth go líonann seilidí teorainneacha na dtéacsanna seo, ach tar éis dom a bheith i mo chónaí in Albain le beagnach bliain anois, táim chun a chreidiúint i dteoiric amháin go háirithe.<br/><br/>Fiú amháin go dtí an lá atá inniu ann, is gnách go mbíonn áitritheoirí na mainistreach ag coinneáil gairdíní. Thar na céadta bliain chuir siad bia agus cógais ar fáil do manaigh, chomh maith le modhanna ó am go chéile chun ioncam beag a ghiniúint chun cabhrú le freastal ar chostais na mainistreach. Ciallaíonn sé seo gur dócha gur chaith na manaigh a chruthaíonn na imealláin seo go leor ama ag coinneáil gairdíní, agus de réir mar a fhios ag aon gharraíodóir, is gníomhaíocht í a thabharfaidh i dteagmháil leat go rialta le seilidí.<br/><br/>Cad ní raibh mé realize – cad nach raibh a chuaigh i ndáiríre i – a bhí díreach cé mhéad seilidí atá anseo.<br/><br/>Tá a lán de na seilidí anseo.<br/><br/>Cosúil – méid ridiculous de seilidí.<br/><br/>Lig mé do Mharkasha amach trí huaire sa lá de ghnáth, agus táimid ag dul díreach isteach sa chlós le feiceáil sna pictiúir éagsúla dá bhfuil mé sa phost. Nuair a théann an ghrian síos, tagann na seilidí amach.<br/><br/>An seilide gníomhach, spéis, scanadh an domhain le haghaidh níos mó plandaí a ithe nó ridirí chun troid.<br/><br/>Tá an seilide seo sa chuid Desert barr an chlóis, ag trasnú an Desert gairbhéil chrua chun an pionta atá mar an bhfaitíos a bhaint amach.Le blianta fada anuas, fuair na ridirí bás, an daonra seilide faoi bhláth, gan a thuilleadh a bheith ar ais ag a gcreachadóirí nádúrtha.<br/><br/>Tá an seilide seo tar éis líne chaonaigh idir brící na siúl.Le dí-speagtha na ridirí, fágadh na manaigh gan chosaint óna bhfaes ársa, rud a d'fhág cogadh díreach idir na hOrduithe Montagacha agus na seilidí.<br/><br/>Seo iad 6 seilide i paiste beag den bhfaiche. Ní raibh dúshlán orthu. Ní raibh na manaigh in ann brath ar na ridirí cosanta a thuilleadh, ní raibh orthu dul i mbun an troid, ag fágáil iad gan mórán ama chun lámhscríbhinní a shoilsiú, agus sin an fáth nach bhfeicimid mórán saothar dá leithéid a thuilleadh.<br/><br/>Tá na cogaí seilide rúnda faoi rún ag bláthanna, mar sin tá sé deacair a fháil amach conas a bhíonn siad ag dul.Dúirt sé sin, tá níos mó seilidí feicthe agam ná manaigh timpeall anseo, mar sin tá sé sábháilte na seilidí a rialú an réigiún seo.<br/><br/>Tá muid beagán gearr ar an ithir, mar sin tá na gairdíní fós sna coimeádáin a d’úsáideamar le haghaidh na síológa. Leis an talamh faoi smacht na seilidí, tá muid go raibh a elevate ár garraíodóireachta. Tá na boscaí fuinneoige in áirithe thar ráille láidir, agus d’fhéadfadh sé a bheith ró-sciliú, ach ní gá dúinn a bheith buartha fúthu ag titim.<br/><br/>Ár barra kale, fós beagán stunned ag easpa ithreach.Beidh muid in ann a cheannach níos luaithe, ach sa Idir an dá linn, tá siad ag déanamh ceart go leor., Níl a fhios ag na seilidí ceachtar, nó atá sásta leis an talamh a rialú beidh sé cabhrú freisin a shábháil mo barra ó na Mollusoid Mainistir. Tá slua de dhíth ar an gcreamhaoiniú, mar sin más féidir leat beagán a chur i dtoll a chéile, cuideodh sé go mór le déileáil!<br/><br/>Blianta ó shin, nuair a bhí mé ag foghlaim ar dtús faoi Phoblacht Dhaonlathach an Phobail Daonlathach na Cóiré, chuala mé faoi theorainneacha dochta a cuireadh i bhfeidhm ar eachtrannaigh a thugann cuairt ar an tír sin. Ní raibh sé ach faoi theorainn a chur leis an méid a dúirt siad agus rinne siad é, ach freisin na míreanna a d’fhéadfadh siad a thabhairt isteach, fóin phóca go háirithe, ba é cuspóir an mhéid sin a rialú conas a chonaic saoránaigh na Cóiré Thuaidh an domhan agus a ról ann. Ba é an dogma bunúsach de ríshliocht Kim gurbh é an Chóiré Thuaidh an náisiún is airde ar domhan, agus cé nach raibh an saol foirfe ann, bhí sé níos fearr ná áit ar bith eile.<br/><br/>Tá mé cinnte nach bhfuil go léir Cóiréis Thuaidh chreidiúint, ach bhí an pointe go leor chun teachtaireacht shoiléir a sheoladh – d'fhéadfadh athrú mar thoradh ar tubaiste., I mo thuairimse, tá tú olc anois? Rock the bád agus beidh sé a fháil níos measa ar do shon. Ba é cuid den bhagairt sin ón rialtas féin, ar ndóigh, ach ag an am céanna níl an smaoineamh go bhfuil an loch, bád contúirteacha lena captaen brúidiúil ar fad atá ar eolas agat riamh, agus nach bhfuil an rogha a chuirtear i láthair idir an bád sin agus bád níos fearr ar chor ar bith.<br/><br/>Agus nuair a dhéanann duine gearán i bhfad, déanann an captaen seó mór a thabhairt don duine sin a chur i gcuimhne do gach duine eile gur féidir le rudaí a bheith níos measa i gcónaí.<br/><br/>Nuair a bhris nuacht na hiarrachtaí ón Rúis cur isteach ar thoghadh uachtaránacht na bliana 2016 sna Stáit Aontaithe, bhí a lán daoine ag iarraidh a bheith ag obair amach cad iad na leasanna atá ag Putin sa mhargú i rialtas Mheiriceá. Ar dtús, le bheith soiléir, is Impireacht beagnach domhanda é Meiriceá, b'fhéidir an chéad cheann dá leithéid a bhaint amach.Clúdaíonn a ráta míleata an phláinéid ar fad trí fhórsa aeir ollmhór, agus líonra ollmhór bunáiteanna míleata. Níor tháinig an chumhacht sin ar fad i dtreo na críche rialaithe, ach i dtreo is go mbeidh an geilleagar domhanda ina chaipiteal, a oiread agus is féidir. Ciallaíonn sé sin go bhfuil bealach an-dáiríre ann ina bhfuil rialú agus gníomhaíochtaí rialtas Mheiriceá ina ábhar imní dlisteanach do gach duine ar an phláinéid.<br/><br/>A chur ar leataobh, bhí ceann de na spreagthaí a mholtar do ghníomhaíochtaí Putin an-chosúil leis an scéal sin a chuala mé faoi An Chóiré Thuaidh. Ní raibh spéis Putin i rialú Mheiriceá, cé gur mhaith leis a bheith gan amhras fíneáil a chumhacht a leathnú, ach i muinín mhuintir na Rúise nach bhféadfadh gach rud a bhí uathu a bheith ag luí leis, ba é an rud is fearr a d'fhéadfadh siad a bheith ag súil leis. Níl aon chóras eile a d'fhéadfadh breathnú níos fearr ón taobh amuigh cobhsaí go leor chun maireachtáil. Tar éis na Stáit Aontaithe oibriú ar bhealach a bheadh go díreach tairbhe a bhaint as Putin deas, ach níos luachmhara ná sin ba é an chaos agus an meath i gcaighdeán maireachtála a thiocfadh ó riarachán Poblachtach faoi Trump. Faoin anailís sin, ní raibh sé cuma an raibh Trump i gceannas go hiomlán, nó fiú gur oibrigh sé i gcónaí chun tairbhe rialtas na Rúise. Ba é an t-ábhar go bhfuil sé fós a bheith deighilte, chaotic, agus creimneach do na Stáit Aontaithe agus a chairde, fianaise a chur ar fáil go raibh an coincheap go Meiriceá daonlathas a éilítear chomh éagobhsaí nach raibh sé fiú ag iarraidh.<br/><br/>Agus mé ag éisteacht leis an bplé seo faoi Putin, agus é a nascadh leis an méid a dúradh liom faoin gCóiré Thuaidh, chuir mé níos mó machnaimh faoi na Stáit Aontaithe, agus na scéalta a chuirtear chugainn mar shaoránaigh na tíre sin. Is féidir le Slogans cosúil le "Meiriceá #1" a fháil i ngach áit, mar is féidir leis an éileamh gurb é an "tír is mó ar domhan", go dtí an méid is cúis le beagán scannal má thugann duine ar bith le fios nach bhfuil an cás. Nuair a labhair muid faoi ár gcóras cúram sláinte a fheabhsú, dúradh linn go raibh an méid a bhí againn cheana féin ar an gcóras cúram sláinte is fearr is féidir, agus go raibh bonn cirt leis na míbhuntáistí, agus go raibh tíortha thar barr amach faoi na rudaí a bhí ag tíortha eile.<br/><br/>Seo ceann de na cúiseanna a mholaim go seasta le haghaidh gníomhaíochta aeráide lena n-áirítear céimeanna drámatúla a ghlacadh chun ullmhú do shaol i bhfad níos teo agus níos intuartha. Dá nglacfaí muid gníomh tromchúiseach chun tomhaltas fuinnimh a laghdú, chun fuinneamh in-athnuaite a fhorbairt, agus chun fuinneamh núicléach a leathnú agus a fheabhsú sna 1970í agus sna 1980idí, bheimis roghanna difriúla ar fáil dúinn inniu, ach chailleamar an fhuinneog sin. Ag filleadh ar an " norm réamhthionsclaíoch", laistigh dár saolré, is cosúil nach bhfuil ann a thuilleadh a bheith ina rogha. Agus mé ag plé i mo phost geoinnealtóireachta, ní mór dúinn foghlaim conas an chumhacht chomhchoiteann atá forbartha againn a úsáid mar speiceas más mian linn domhan inmharthana do na glúnta atá le teacht a úsáid go freagrach.<br/><br/>Ba é Gerry Stanhill, eolaí Sasanach atá ag obair in Iosrael, a chuir iontas ar Stanfield titim mhór a aimsiú sa radaíocht gréine. "Bhí titim stogering 22% i solas na gréine, agus go bhfuil iontas mór orm," a deir sé.<br/><br/>Fuair sé taifid ó gach cearn den domhan agus fuair sé an scéal céanna beagnach i ngach áit, agus bhí 10% os cionn SAM ag titim sna solas solas solas, beagnach 30% i gcodanna den Aontas Sóivéadach a bhí ann roimhe seo, agus fiú ag 16% i gcodanna de na hOileáin Breataine. Cé gur éagsúil an éifeacht go mór ó áit go háit, ba é an laghdú 1-2% ar fud an domhain idir na 1950í agus na 1990í an laghdú iomlán.<br/><br/>D'fhoilsigh Gerry an fheiniméan diminsean domhanda, ach a chuid taighde, le freagairt amhrasach ó eolaithe eile. Ní raibh sé ach le déanaí, nuair a dhearbhaigh eolaithe na hAstráile a chonclúidí ag baint úsáide as modh go hiomlán difriúil chun radaíocht na gréine a mheas, dhúisigh eolaithe aeráide ar deireadh suas go dtí réaltacht an de bharr an dhimtheachta dhomhanda.<br/><br/>Is cosúil go bhfuil demitming de bharr truailliú aeir. Táirgeann gual, ola agus adhmad, cibé acu i gcarranna, stáisiúin chumhachta nó i dteinteáin cócaireachta, ní hamháin dé-ocsaíd charbóin dhofheicthe (príomhgháis an tí atá freagrach as téamh domhanda) ach freisin cáithníní aeir bídeacha de thó, de thacaí, de chomhdhúile sulfair agus de thruailleáin eile.<br/><br/>Léiríonn an truailliú aeir infheicthe seo solas na gréine ar ais isteach sa spás, rud a choisceann sé an dromchla a bhaint amach. Ach athraíonn an truailliú airíonna optúla na scamaill freisin. Toisc go síníonn na cáithníní foirmiú braoiníní uisce, tá líon níos mó braoiníní i scamaill truaillithe ná mar atá sna scamaill neamhthruaillithe. Léiríonn taighde le déanaí go ndéanann sé seo níos machnamh orthu ná mar a bheadh siad murach sin, ag léiriú arís ar ghhathanna na Gréine ar ais isteach sa spás.<br/><br/>A mhéid a dhéanann aerasóil fionnuar síos ár gcomhshaol? Go dtí seo, bhí gach meastachán neamhiontaofa toisc go raibh sé dodhéanta éifeachtaí na gaoithe atá ag ardú a chruthaíonn na scamaill a scaradh, ó éifeachtaí aerasóil a chinneann a gcomhdhéanamh. Go dtí seo.<br/><br/>D’fhorbair Rosenfeld agus a chomhghleacaí Yanan Zhu ó Institiúid Meitéareolaíochta Cúige Shaanxi sa tSín modh nua a úsáideann íomhánna satailíte chun éifeacht na gaoithe ingearach agus uimhreacha droplet scamall a ríomh ar leithligh. Chuir siad an mhodheolaíocht seo i bhfeidhm chun scamall íseal-bhreathnaithe a chlúdach os cionn aigéin an domhain idir an Meánchiorcal agus 40S. Leis an modh nua seo, bhí Rosenfeld agus a chomhghleacaithe in ann éifeachtaí fuaraithe aerasóil níos cruinne ar bhuiséad fuinnimh an Domhain a ríomh. Agus fuair siad amach go bhfuil éifeacht fuaraithe aerasóil beagnach dhá uair níos airde ná mar a measadh roimhe seo.<br/><br/>Mar sin féin, má tá sé seo fíor ansin conas a thagann an domhan ag éirí níos teo, ní níos fuaire?I gcás gach aird dhomhanda ar théamh aeráide, tá rátaí truaillithe aerasóil ó fheithiclí, ó thalmhaíocht agus plandaí cumhachta fós an-ard.<br/><br/>Ós rud é go bhfuil ár bplainéad ag fáil níos teo cé go bhfuil aerasóil á fhuarú síos ag rátaí níos airde ná mar a shíl muid a ghabhann le staid Catch-22: d'fhéadfadh iarrachtaí domhanda chun feabhas a chur ar cháilíocht aeir trí bhreoslaí níos glaine a fhorbairt agus níos lú guail a dhó dochar a dhéanamh dár bplainéad trí líon na n-aerasóil san atmaisféar a laghdú, agus tríd sin a dhéanamh, cumas fuaraithe aerasóil a laghdú chun téamh domhanda a fhritháireamh.<br/><br/>De réir Rosenfeld, is hipitéis eile chun a mhíniú cén fáth go bhfuil an Domhan ag fáil aerairiscí níos teo cé go bhfuil sé fuaraithe ag ráta níos mó fós is féidir éifeacht téimh aerasóil nuair a bhíonn siad i scamaill domhain, rud a chiallaíonn na 10 ciliméadar nó níos mó ná an Domhan. Tá an Ghníomhaireacht Spáis agus Lárionad Náisiúnta an Fhrainc um Staidéar Spáis (CNES) i gcomhar le chéile chun teacht ar shatailítí nua a bheidh in ann an feiniméan domhain scamall seo a fhiosrú, agus an tOllamh Rosenfeld ina phríomh-imscrúdaitheoir.<br/><br/>Ar an mbealach céanna, tá an chonclúid mar an gcéanna.Ní chuireann ár tuartha aeráide domhanda reatha san áireamh i gceart éifeachtaí suntasacha aerasóil ar scamaill ar chothromaíocht fuinnimh foriomlán an Domhain. Ina theannta sin, is é atá i gceist le fritháirimh Rosenfeld ná go n-athmhacnófaí a dtreochtaí domhanda téimh — rud a thuar faoi láthair méadú teochta 1.5 go 4.5 céim Celsius faoi dheireadh an 21ú haois — diagnóis níos cruinne a sholáthar dúinn — agus an prognóis — de chuid an Domhain.<br/><br/>Is córas polaitiúil agus eacnamaíoch é an rud nach bhfuil againn faoi láthair a ligeann dúinn freagairt d’éilimh ár gcuid ama. Ní féidir linn táirgeadh domhanda, dáileadh, agus gluaiseacht an duine a bheith ordaithe ag agus chun leasa codán beag bídeach den chine daonna. Leis an tiomáint ar mhaithe le fás gan deireadh, agus carnadh caipitil gan deireadh, fiú má bhí córas níos forásaí againn maidir le cánachas agus athdháil talún, cruthaítear ganntanas saorga, agus cuirtear cosc ar fhorbairt sochaí is féidir a chothú san fhadtéarma.<br/><br/>Ní hé seo ach éigeandáil a chruthaíonn ár n-úsáid fuinnimh, tá sé fréamhaithe freisin sa chóras caipitil a rialaíonn beagnach gach gníomhaíocht dhaonna ar an bpláinéad seo. Teastaíonn daonlathas radacach sa pholaitíocht agus sa gheilleagar uainn má táimid chun aon dóchas a bheith againn aghaidh a thabhairt ar ár ngéarchéim chomhshaoil, agus ar an gcine daonna a shábháil. Tá mé ag déanamh ionadaíochta air anseo mar is dóigh liom gur fiú a bheith thart, agus toisc go bhfuil sé ábhartha do phost táim ag obair faoi láthair maidir le dimadh domhanda.<br/><br/>Le blianta fada anuas, tá ceannairí gnó agus polaitiúla an domhain tar éis teacht de réir a chéile chun a thuiscint go bhfuil an t-athrú aeráide rud éigin nach féidir neamhaird a dhéanamh air. Gach bliain, tá an t-inmhiúlacht agus déine na faidhbe níos soiléire. Mar gheall ar ardú ar leibhéal na farraige, athruithe séasúracha, agus fiú athruithe éabhlóideacha mar fhreagairt ar an ardú ar theocht phláinéid tá sé soiléir go bhfuil an phláinéid ar fad ag athrú thart orainn, agus go neamhaird d'fhéadfadh sé torthaí tubaisteacha a bheith aige.<br/><br/>Mar a dhéanaimid, i sochaí a chuireann luach ar airgead chomh mór sin, bhí cuid de na freagraí intuartha go háirithe, tá an gnó cosúil le Bill Gates ag brú an smaoineamh ar innealtóireacht geo-eolaíochta mar réiteach. Is frása ar fad é geo-innealtóireacht, sa chomhthéacs seo le haghaidh tinneála d'aon ghnó le haeráid an Domhain agus na meicníochtaí a théann i bhfeidhm air.Is í an fhadhb leis seo ná go bhfuil an téarma chomh leathan sin gan úsáid. Is féidir é a chur i bhfeidhm ar rudaí cosúil le plandáil níos mó crainn, agus is féidir é a chur i bhfeidhm freisin ar struchtúir colossal sa spás chun laghdú ar an solas isteach.<br/><br/>Ceann de na réitigh geo-innealtóireachta is coitianta a phléitear ná leasachán iarann na farraige. Is é an smaoineamh bunúsach simplí – is cothaithigh teoranta san aigéan é iarann, mar sin spreagfaidh cáithníní iarainn san aigéan fás an phótocsainéis, a tharraingeoidh CO2 amach as an atmaisféar. Is é an smaoineamh ná nuair a gheobhaidh an planctóin bás, go ndéanfaidh méid suntasach dá mais dul go bun na farraige ag glacadh an charbóin sin leis.<br/><br/>Léiríonn taighde nua-fhoilsithe anois toisc nach é iarann an t-aon chothaithigh atá ar fáil in aigéan, d’fhéadfadh an t- bhláth alóideach ó thréifliú iarainn in aon chuid de na haigéin cothaithigh eile a tharraingt, cosúil le níotráití agus fosfáití, as an uisce, ag mealladh planctóin níos faide srutha feadh na sruthanna farraige.<br/><br/>Tá sé tempting go simplí tonn tú ag innealtóireachta mar smaoineamh dona gur chóir dúinn a adhlacadh agus a dhéanamh leis. Tá bealaí gan áireamh ann go bhféadfadh sé dul mícheart go dona, go háirithe nuair a a achtaitheodh billionaires cosúil le Gates agus a chuid fírinne, nach bhfuil aon tuiscint acu ar na héiceachórais ar mian leo cur isteach orthu. Cé is moite de phlandáil níos mó crainn agus spásanna níos fiáine a chruthú (rud a d'fhéadfadh a bheith ag obair, go leor gach moladh do geoninnealtóireacht fo-iarsmaí tubaisteach a d'fhéadfadh an saol a dhéanamh ar an Domhan i bhfad níos deacra.<br/><br/>Tá cúis láidir amháin ann gan é a chaitheamh ar fad.Is í an réaltacht go bhfuilimid ag gabháil do geoinnealtóireacht cheana féin, agus níl aon cheist ann go dtiocfaidh deireadh leis an gcosán atá á dhéanamh againn faoi láthair go dona. Cosúil leis nó nach bhfuil, tá an chine daonna ina fhórsa nádúir. Ciallaíonn méid ár ndaonra agus scála ár dteicneolaíocht go bhfuil tionchar domhanda againn ar chomhdhéanamh ceimiceach aigéin, atmaisféar, talún ár bpláinéad. Faoi láthair, táimid ag gabháil do chineál na geo-innealtóireacht a rinneadh thar aois ó shin - teocht an phláinéid a ardú trí thiúis gás ceaptha teasa a mhéadú.<br/><br/>Ar mhaithe lenár marthanais fadtéarmach féin, gan an chuid eile den saol ar an Domhan a lua, ní mór dúinn téarmaí a chruthú leis an bhfíric go bhfuil tionchar domhanda ag ár speiceas, agus ní mór dúinn smacht d'aon ghnó a fháil ar an tionchar sin. Tá muid cheana féin geo-innealtóirí, nach bhfuil muid ag glacadh ach freagracht as é. Tá sé thar am níos mó ná obair a dhéanamh ar laghdú a dhéanamh ar ár n-úsáid breosla iontaise – ní mór dúinn smaoineamh ar an gcaoi a ndéanaimid bainistiú ar dhromchla an phláinéid ina gcónaíonn muid, agus conas is féidir linn é a bhainistiú ar mhaithe leis an saol ar fad ar an Domhan – muid féin san áireamh.<br/><br/>Mar gheall ar an gceart anois, is cosúil go bhfuilimid fós ag ligean orthu gur féidir linn stop a chur le tionchar pláinéadach a bheith againn, agus lenár ndaonra atá i gceannas ar feadh 10 mbilliún i gceann cúpla bliain, is é sin an rogha amháin nach bhfuil ar fáil a thuilleadh d'ainm mo bhlag, tá roinnt spéise agam i méid ocsaigine díscaoilte san aigéan. Ní ábhar é a bhfuil aon saineolas ar leith agam ann, níl ann ach rud a d'fhéadfadh tionchar drámatúil a bheith aige ar éiceachórais uisceacha, agus a bhaineann le ceann de na "chásanna nightmare" féideartha le haghaidh téamh domhanda.<br/><br/>Maidir le bunfhíricí an athraithe aeráide, tá roinnt rudaí i ndáiríre simplí go leor, in ainneoin chastacht iomlán na saincheiste. Ciallaíonn airíonna CO2, mar a thuairiscítear i 1856 go dtiocfaidh méadú ar leibhéil CO2 san atmaisféar de bharr na teochta domhanda.Is í an t-aon cheist atá ann ná an méid. D’fhreagair Arrhenius an cheist sin in 1896 le ríomh ginearálta ar an méid a chuirfí isteach ar athrú ar leith in CO2 a bheadh ag teacht ar theocht fhoriomlán an Domhain. Mar sin tá a fhios againn go méadaíonn leibhéil CO2 méadaithe teocht an phláinéid, agus tá a fhios againn go garbh cé mhéad a gheobhaidh muid ó mhéadú ar leith.Níl neamhchinnteacht ann, i bpáirt mar gheall ar chastacht chóras aeráide an Domhain. Má dhéantar lúba a chur i bhfeidhm ar an aiseolas, is féidir leibhéil gáis cheaptha teasa a mhéadú, agus lúba ar gcúl a chur faoi chois, is féidir iad a laghdú.<br/><br/>Mar an gcéanna, má ardaíonn an teocht, beidh uisce in ann ocsaigin nach bhfuil chomh htuaslagtha a shealbhú.Is é seo an rud atá ar eolas againn le fada an lá, cé go raibh níos mó deacracht agam an taighde is luaithe a rianú air. Na tagairtí is luaithe a fuair mé go dona, ach sna 1940idí.Má tá níos mó sonraí ag aon duine faoi sin, beidh fáilte romhat an bhreis sin a dhéanamh!<br/><br/>Is féidir linn tosú le criosanna marbha. Is limistéar é crios marbh i gcorp uisce ina bhfuil na leibhéil ocsaigine tuaslagtha ró-íseal chun tacú leis an saol is ilcheallach.Tá roinnt díobh seo, atá níos mó agus níos poiblíochtaí ar fud chóstaí an domhain. Is é an chuid is mó de bharr gníomhaíocht an duine.Níl gach ceann acu ann ar feadh na bliana, ach tá éifeachtaí ídiú ocsaigine bliantúil amuigh faoin ídiú féin. De ghnáth bíonn eotrófaíocht de thoradh orthu sin (méadú ar chothaithigh a theorannú) de bharr rónghlanadh feirme atá ag sileadh isteach san fharraige. Is dócha gurb é an ceann a chruthaíonn timpeall an díle Mississippi gach earrach ar an gcúis seo an ceann is cáiliúla sna Stáit Aontaithe is cúis le héagóir faoi bhláth algaí, a d'fhéadfadh méadú gairid ar ocsaigin i rith an lae, ach ansin san oíche, ag fáil algaí tarraing an t-aer amach as an seomra, mar sin a labhairt. Má tá siad in ann a fhágáil ar an limistéar., Mura féidir leo, mar gheall ar an méid de, nó toisc nach féidir leo a insint ar bhealach chun dul, beidh siad bás.<br/><br/>Tá sé tábhachtach a thabhairt faoi deara nach bhfuil na criosanna marbh seo mar thoradh ar mhéadú ar theocht, agus go n-ardóidh na leibhéil ocsaigine tar éis don líon cothaitheach stadanna isteach, agus go dtéann an t- bhláth algach amach. D’fhéadfadh an laghdú ar ocsaigin mar gheall ar theochtaí a ardú tosú ag taispeáint i bpatrúin séasúracha, ach ní bheidh sé chomh drámatúil. Bogann líonra na sruthaí ar fud chóras farraige an phláinéid an iomarca uisce chun staid dhomhanda na hanocsa a cheadú ag pointe ar bith go luath. Mar a scríobh mé ag tús mo ghairm bheatha blagála, tá bealaí ann ina bhféadfadh tromlach na farraige deireadh a chur leo mar chrios marbh.Tá an cás sin ar cheann de na príomhfhachtóirí a d’fhéadfadh a bheith sa mhór-thuilleamh mór is mó sa taifead geolaíochta. Chuirfeadh teochtaí atá ag ardú leis an tarlú, ar ndóigh, ach ní fhéadfaidís a bheith ina gcúis leo féin gan athrú suntasach a dhéanamh ar an láthair aigéan dhomhanda a bheith ag teastáil chun stop a chur le meascadh idir sraitheanna na farraige.<br/><br/>Ó alt Livescience nasctha leis an íomhá: Tá limistéir íseal-ocsaigin ag leathadh ar fud na cruinne. Seasann poncanna dearga ar láithreacha cósta ina bhfuil ocsaigin tar éis 2 mhuilleog in aghaidh an lítir nó níos lú, agus ina bhfuil limistéir ghorma ag teacht leis na leibhéil chéanna ocsaigine san aigéan oscailte. Sonraí ón Aigéan Domhanda, Atlas 2013 agus arna soláthar ag R. J. Diaz)<br/><br/>Mar sin, cad is dócha go bhfeicfimid inár saolré?Tá luas an téimh ag méadú le blianta anuas, agus leanfaidh sé sin ag caitheamh tamall go dtí go gcaithfimid an t-athrú aeráide mar a bhíonn sé. Mar thoradh ar athruithe móra a dhéanamh ar an gcaoi a n-úsáidtear leasacháin sa talmhaíocht, leanfaidh criosanna marbh ag de bheith ina rud, agus is dócha go rachaidh siad níos measa le teochtaí níos airde. Thug an crios hypoxic san Atlantach Meánchiorcal léargas dúinn ar na chéad éifeachtaí a bhaineann le ocsaigin a laghdú:<br/><br/>Tá crios ocsaigine íseal ag leathnú, ar a dtugtar crios hypoxic, san Aigéan Atlantach ag méadú ar an ngnáthóg ab flúirseach ab fhearr le hocsaigin, ag cur i mbaol dóibh in uiscí saoir ina bhfuil siad níos mó seans go ngabhfar iad.<br/><br/>Le linn an staidéir, a foilsíodh le déanaí san iris Fish seaagrafaíocht, rinne eolaithe clibeáil 79 iasc goirt agus marrlin gorm le feistí rianaithe satailíte in iarthar an Atlantaigh Thuaidh, i ndeisceart Florida agus sa Mhuir Chairib, agus in oirthear an Atlantaigh thróiciúla, amach ó chósta Iarthar na hAfraice. Dheimhnigh taighdeoirí gur fearr le billí iascaigh uiscí saibhre níos gaire don dromchla agus go seachnóidh siad uiscí íseal i ocsaigin go gníomhach.<br/><br/>Cé go dtarlaíonn na criosanna hipiciúla seo go nádúrtha i go leor réimsí d’aigéan trópaiceach agus Mheánchriosacha an domhain, tá imní ar eolaithe toisc go bhfuil na criosanna sin ag leathnú agus go bhfuil siad ag tarlú níos gaire do dhromchla na farraige, agus go bhfuiltear ag súil go leanfaidh siad ag fás de réir mar ardaíonn teochtaí farraige.<br/><br/>Is é an "crios hypoxic of West Africa, a chlúdaíonn beagnach gach uiscí meánchiorcal san Aigéan Atlantach, méid na mór-roinne Aontaithe, agus tá sé ag fás," a dúirt an Dr Eric D. Prince, bitheolaí taighde seirbhíse Iascaigh an NAA. Agus an timthriall reatha d’athrú aeráide agus an téamh domhanda luathaithe againn, táimid ag súil le méid an limistéir seo a mhéadú, agus an ghnáthóg atá ar fáil don iasc seo a laghdú tuilleadh."<br/><br/>Is féidir go mbeidh níos mó iasc á ghabháil mar thoradh ar ghnáthóg níos lú ós rud é go bhfuil an t-iasc tiubhaithe in aice leis an dromchla. D'fhéadfadh rátaí gabhála níos airde a bheith mar thoradh ar chuma bréagach stoic iasc níos flúirseach. Is tosca tábhachtacha iad infhaighteacht géaraithe gnáthóige agus méaduithe ar rátaí gabhála agus measúnuithe daonra á ndéanamh acu.<br/><br/>Is dócha gurb é seo an príomhéifeacht a fheicimid inár saolré.D’fhéadfadh sé a bheith mar phríomhathruithe éisc agus patrúin a a athraíonn de réir mar a bhíonn an t-iasc ag dul ó roghanna teochta, bogfaidh siad mar fhreagra ar leibhéil ocsaigine atá ag athrú, agus ar ghníomhaíochtaí éisc eile freisin. In éineacht le bagairt shíor-theachtrach na ró-iascaireacht, d’fhéadfadh roinnt titimeanna iascaigh a bheith mar thoradh air seo go luath, rud a d’fhéadfadh tionchar a imirt ar an soláthar bia domhanda ag an am céanna a bhfuil ár gcórais talmhaíochta ag streachailt le hathruithe ar phatrúin aimsire agus infhaighteacht uisce.<br/><br/>Céard atá níos mó, fuair a gcuid staidéir go ndéanann slabhra na beatha mara atá ancaire ag phytoplancton – infheistíocht timpeall faoin gcéad (1ratestat) dá fhortún cumhachta ceimiceach i bhfuinneamh meicniúil, a léirítear i tairiscint snámha snámha snámh snámh snámh snámh snámh snámh snámh snámhóirí aigéin ó mhíolta móra agus iasc go ribí róibéis agus fuarú. Measc an t-uisce mar a dhéantar an t-uisce a ghreanadh ina caife trí spúnóg a mhalartú tríd.<br/><br/>Agus d'fhéadfadh suim gach corraithe a bhí breosla fitaplanctóin a rialú aeráide comhionann.<br/><br/>Trí na sonraí atá ann cheana a léirmhíniú ar bhealach difriúil, ní mór dúinn a thuar teoiriciúil go bhfuil an méid a mheascadh ba chúis leis an snámhoirí farraige domhain inchomparáide leis an mheascadh farraige ba chúis leis an ghaoth ag séideadh ar dhromchla na farraige agus éifeachtaí na taoide," a dúirt Dewar.<br/><br/>Go deimhin, is cosúil go gcuireann sé ar fáil faoi aon trian den chumhacht a theastaíonn chun an domhan a thabhairt chun an dromchla, uiscí fuara an aigéin a chríochnaíonn, agus é ríthábhachtach don chóras aeráide domhanda ina dhiaidh sin.<br/><br/>Beidh torthaí ón staidéar faoi stiúir FOSU ("An bhfuil an meascán bith-thoise muirí an aigéin?") le feiceáil san eagrán atá le teacht ar an Iris Taighde Mara, ag cur ról an bhróicéara mhór chumhachta le dintiúir shuntasacha an fíteashláintíocha cheana féin.<br/><br/>Tá a fhios ag eolaithe le tamall go ngníomhaíonn na plandaí an-íogair mar chomharthaí iontaofa d’athruithe comhshaoil ag an aigéan nó in aice leis trí mheath tobann nó fás tapa - agus tá amhras orthu go dtéann fíteasplanctóin i bhfeidhm ar an athrú aeráide chomh maith le léargas a thabhairt ar athrú aeráide nuair a bhíonn líon mór, daonraí inbhuanaithe ag dul ó dhé-ocsaíd charbóin an atmaisféar le linn grianghrafadóireacht ar scála mór.<br/><br/>Ach mar aon leis na ríomhanna nua a dhíríonn ar ról níos mó ná mar a bhíothas ag súil leis i meascadh aigéan agus rialú aeráide, molann Deladar agus a chomhghleacaithe go bhféadfadh tionchar intomhaiste a bheith ag díchumadh éisc agus ag pobail mhóra ar an mbithsreabhú iomlán in aigéin an domhain.<br/><br/>Ní bheidh laghdú ar ocsaigin díscaoilte ar an mbaol is mó a bhíonn ar an éiceachóras aigéin ó uisce téimh, ach déanann sé damáiste don phlandóin a chruthaíonn an bonn don chuid is mó de na gréasáin bia mara. D’fhéadfadh iasc mór a bheith ag iasc mór cosúil le tuinnín a bheith tiubhaithe ar shiúl ó chriosanna hypoxic, ach bheadh fadhb ag an meath i mbia atá ar fáil fiú gan athruithe ar leibhéil ocsaigine.<br/><br/>Is dócha go dtabharfaidh meath an phleantóin líon méadaithe ceannlínte dúinn maidir le táirgeadh ocsaigine a laghdú ó na haigéin, mar léiríonn an t-alt atá nasctha agam. É sin ráite, ní hé an baol ó sin, i mo mheastachán, go mbeidh ocsaigin á rith againn chun an t-anáil a dhéanamh, ach go gcaillfidh muid seirbhís éiceachórais iontógáil CO2 a sholáthraíonn phytoplankton.<br/><br/>Is é an turgnamh terraming domhanda atá á reáchtáil againn faoi láthair beagán intinn-boggling, i ndáiríre. Nuair a bheidh muid ag ceannlínte thar an mbarr, tá siad sensational i bhfócas, ní ar scála - imní faoi rith as ocsaigin an chéid seo, nó an scéal a thug breith ar an teideal an bhlag seo nach bhfuil réasúnta i ár saolré, agus go bhfuil mé buíoch. Tá an réaltacht, áfach, chomh drámatúil, agus cé go bhfuil sé deacair a thuar go díreach conas a rachaidh rudaí a imirt amach, sílim go bhfuil sé sábháilte a rá go leanfaidh sé a bhraitheann cosúil go bhfuil muid gafa i timthriall de chaos a ardú.<br/><br/>Toisc go bhfuil muid Alexandra Ocasio-Corez figiúr spreagúil ar roinnt cúiseanna, ach is é mo thuairimse, an ceann is mó ná a tiomantas chun brath ar dheontóirí beag-dollar, agus cur chuige "sneaicéirí ar an talamh" i leith feachtas. Ar thaobh amháin, tá sé deas a bhraitheann go bhfuil roinnt reachtóirí nach bhfuil aon rud dlite do na daoine uaisle, ach is dóigh liom go dtéann sé níos faide ná sin.<br/><br/>Ní chiallaíonn na síntiúis mhóra dollar sin go léir go bhfuil na polaiteoirí "weo" a ndeontóirí, is méid ollmhór ama iad ag glacadh an airgid sin. Sin an t-am a chaitheann siad ag caint nó ag éisteacht le comhábhair. Sin am nach gcaitear ag déanamh staidéir ar na hábhair a bhfuil siad ag dul dlíthe a scríobh., Sin an t-am sin nach gcaitear ag déanamh taighde ar na daoine a bhfuil siad ag dul a bheith ag caint leo in éisteachtaí caonta.<br/><br/>Tá sé ag athnuachan, le blianta beaga anuas, baill den Chomhdháil mar AOC, Katie Porter, Ilhan Omar, Ayanna Pressley, agus daoine eile ag iarraidh ceisteanna deacra, ceisteanna eolasacha faoi chinn ghníomhaireachta, fheidhmeanna corparáideacha, agus daoine eile a thugtar isteach faoin ngiúise de chúnamh a thabhairt don Chomhdháil iad féin faoi na dlíthe a bhíonn siad ag crafting agus ag dul thar a chéile.<br/><br/>Ní dóigh liom gur chomhtharlú é gur cosúil go bhfuil na ceisteanna sin ag teacht den chuid is mó ó bhaill níos clé den Pháirtí Daonlathach, agus is gnách go ndéanann na ceisteanna brú níos lú agus níos mó airde a bheith ag féachaint orthu. Baineann cuid de sin le tosaíochtaí, ar ndóigh, níl na spriocanna céanna ag polaiteoirí sa cheart ar an speictream polaitiúil do nádúr agus éifeachtacht rialtas na Stát Aontaithe.<br/><br/>Mar chuid de, áfach, téann sé ar ais go dtí an t-am a chaitear ag tiomsú. Ní hamháin go bhfuil AOC níos mó eolach go nádúrtha faoi na hábhair sin. Tá sí féin agus a comhghleacaithe chomh héifeachtach toisc go gcuireann siad an t-am i ndáiríre ag déanamh staidéir ar na hábhair, agus ag éisteacht le saineolaithe, agus ag déanamh ceisteanna úsáideacha a cheardú. Ní hamháin go dtugann a gcur chuige i leith maoiniú a gcuid feachtas polaitiúil buntáistí stairiúla do dhaoine i ndiaidh an phobail, ceannaíonn sé iad freisin an t-am a theastaíonn chun a bheith éifeachtach ar a gcuid post.<br/><br/>Sílim go bhfuil ceachtanna ann do dhaoine lasmuigh de réimse na polaitíochta.Mar a luaigh mé cheana, cosnaíonn úsáid cumhachta den scoth níos mó againn, agus tá sé níos éagsúla ná sin go n-úsáidtear cumhacht chaipitil. Tógann sé níos mó ama, iarrachta, agus íobairt chun ár gcumhacht a thabhairt chun iompróidh agus a choinneáil dírithe ar na tascanna atá ar láimh.Déanann sé sin an réiteach “éasca” ar mhaoiniú caipitil a ghlacadh an-, an-temptáil. Ar an dromchla, ar a laghad, is féidir le hairgead a thugann roinnt eintiteas mór caipitil, bíodh sé ina eagraíocht nó ina dhuine aonair, rudaí a dhéanamh i bhfad níos éasca.Is féidir leis acmhainní a bhfuil géarghá leo a chur ar fáil i dtreo cúiseanna maithe. Is féidir leis tionchar truaillithe a bheith aige freisin, áfach, seachas gnáthscéal daoine a fháil greedy, agus radharc a chailliúint ar an misean.<br/><br/>Is féidir le maoiniú ollmhór ó líon beag foinsí saibhre "go maith a chiallaíonn, ar an dromchla, tá tuiscint ann go bhfaigheann am agus iarracht na síntiúis mhóra sin a fháil ar ais níos fearr don chúis, agus is féidir leis sin, leis an gcuid is fearr d'intinn, gnéithe ár n-eagraíochtaí a bhfuil maoiniú á lorg ní hamháin acu ó "na daoine" a chur i dteagmháil leis na daoine sin freisin.<br/><br/>Agus in am, is dóichí nach bhfeicimid caidreamh pátrúnachta buan, a bheith ag brath ar thacaíocht an ranga caipitil/eoráide, ach is minic a bhíonn gach rud ag brath air.<br/><br/>Tá ceist na n-acmhainní ábhartha thar a bheith tábhachtach d’aon réabhlóid, is cuma má tá an réabhlóid sin foréigneach nó neamhghéarthábhachtach. Ar an drochuair, áit a dtagann na hacmhainní sin ó thaobh ábhair nach bhfuil íonacht luachmhar iontu.<br/><br/>Tá an físeán seo ó Seac Comen isteach ar na ceisteanna seo ar bhealach a chuireann ar mo thuairimse, gur gá breathnú ar dhuine ar bith a chreideann gur chóir do dhaoine a bheith in ann iad féin a rialú: shroich beag bhaile Seirbis thuaidh an Chiorcail Artach 100.4 Fahrenheit ar an Satharn, figiúr sin - dá mba é an léamh teocht is airde sa réigiún a thosaigh taifead i 1885. Le gach an chaos agus an trua de na ceithre bliana seo caite, tá sé deacair gan mothú amhail is dá bhfuil muid ag dul isteach sa "ceirdeachas aeráide" ré eagla mé i gcónaí. Bhí sé soiléir i gcónaí go bhfuil an aeráid ag téamh níos tapúla ná mar a thuar formhór na n-eolaithe (in ainneoin an méid a choinníonn na dearthóirí eolaíochta ag rá), agus tá a fhios agam i gcónaí, i gcúl m’intinn, go mbeadh téamh domhanda agus chaos a bhaineann le chéile an "suíomh" don dara leath de mo shaol. Ba é an t-ionchas gruama go mbeidh mé dírithe go ginearálta ar an dóchas caol go bhféadfaí é a sheachaint, ach ní dóigh liom gur rún é go rabhamar ag súil go gcoinneodh muid ag titim gearr de na hathruithe éagsúla a theastaíonn.<br/><br/>Tá córais an Domhain gan cothromaíocht cheana féin. Ciallaíonn an eiceamhéid chomparáideach a chonaic muid le linn an chuid is mó de na 10,000 bliain seo caite go raibh an méid oighir a bhí againn go mór mór leis an méid oighir a bhí an-dóchúil againn a fháil agus a choinneáil ag ár leibhéal teocht reatha agus gáis cheaptha teasa. Nuair a mhéadaigh muid an meánteocht, aistríodh an t-iarmhéid sin, agus thosaigh oighear ag leá mar fhreagairt ar theocht mhéadaithe na haeráide.<br/><br/>An "oll" idir 1998 agus 2015, nach raibh i bhfad de lull, chonaic fós ag luasghéarú leá oighir, permafrost leá, agus leibhéal na farraige ardú, mar gheall ar a raibh muid a ardaíodh cheana féin ar an teocht go leor chun iad siúd dosheachanta a dhéanamh, bunaithe ar ár tuiscint ar fhisic. Fiú sa bhliain a bhí síos go dtí leibhéal teochta 1990 nó 1980, ar an meán, agus ina dhiaidh sin ba bheag éifeacht a bheadh ag filleadh ar theocht 2000s. Bheadh an leá mall, gan stopadh, agus ansin dlús arís nuair a d'fhill an teocht go dtí an decal " norm".<br/><br/>Ach bliain níos teo go mór – mar seo tá an bhliain El Niño - ábhar difriúil. Tá sé á instealladh a bunch níos mó teas isteach sa chóras, rud a chiallaíonn leá oighir níos tapúla, agus mar sin níos ísle cé go bhfuil sé don bhliain atá le teacht, agus mar sin níos mó permafrost, leá gáis cheaptha teasa don bhliain atá le teacht, agus mar sin níos mó galú uisce, agus mar sin gáis cheaptha teasa don bhliain atá le teacht.<br/><br/>Bliain amháin fuar de ghnáth, ní dhéanann sé i bhfad nuair a bhíonn muid fós os cionn an teocht ag a bhfuil na bileoga oighir reatha déanta, ach is féidir le bliain te amháin a chruthú le spíc de fachtóirí téamh, a bheidh ina chúis le téamh níos mó sna blianta amach romhainn.<br/><br/>Más rud é nach raibh muid ag fáil réidh le breoslaí iontaise, is féidir go mbeadh níos mó fuaraithe domhanda mar thoradh ar an snámh i dteocht dhomhanda ag deireadh na 1960idí/na 1970idí, agus fiú aois oighir – táimid cinnte mar gheall ar cheann amháin – ach bhí próiseas an téimh dhomhanda ag luasghéarú go mall againn cheana féin.
